romania.title: Centrele Generații – o inovație socială în educația intergenerațională: seniorii devin mentori pentru copiii în risc de abandon școlar
romania.summary: De Ziua Europeană a Solidarității între Generații, Fundația Regală Margareta a României reafirmă importanța conexiunilor intergeneraționale pentru construirea unui viitor durabil pentru toate vârstele și invită seniorii să se alăture… Articolul Centrele Generații – o inovație socială în educația intergenerațională: seniorii devin mentori pentru copiii în risc de abandon școlar apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Copii și adulți se… Articolul 10 (zece) ani Satul meșteșugurilor | Concert aniversar Ada Milea apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: FOTO Operă a lui Brâncuși, în fața recordului – poate ajunge la pragul de 100 milioane dolari. A fost pusă în licitație
romania.summary: O sculptură de Brâncuși din seria Danaide va fi pusă în vânzare la New York. Lucrarea este una din cele 16 capodopere din licitația unei colecții a unui magnat media american și poate stabili un record fără precedent pentru o sculptură și pentru Brâncuși, respectiv 100 milioane dolari. Este vorba de una din lucrările din …

romania.title: CCR a declarat neconstituțională legea care limitează numărul jucătorilor străini în sporturile de echipă
romania.summary: Curtea Constituţională a declarat neconstituţional, miercuri, proiectul de lege care prevede obligaţia federaţiilor sportive naţionale de a stabili o pondere a participării sportivilor de performanţă români în competiţiile sportive naţionale oficiale, la sporturile de echipă, care nu poate fi mai mică de 40% din totalul de sportivi participanţi, informează News.ro. Curtea Constituţională, cu majoritate de …

romania.title: Auchan România la 20 de ani: un retailer multiformat cu  peste 500 de magazine, prezent în toată țara
romania.summary: Auchan România marchează două decenii de comerț sustenabil în serviciul clienților, de susținere și dezvoltare a producătorilor români și promovare a produselor românești, de investiții și inovație, construind una dintre cele mai relevante rețele comerciale multiformat din retailul local. În acești 20 de ani, compania a evoluat în linie cu schimbările din societate, economie și comportamentul de consum, de la un model centrat pe hipermarket la un ecosistem complex multicanal de soluții integrate pentru clienți și parteneri, păstrând un fir roșu conducător - prețurile mici.

romania.title: 😂 Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
romania.summary: Lansat în 2006 ca o satiră SF a unei lumi posibile, dar improbabile, filmul Idiocracy a ajuns să fie perceput azi cu totul altfel. Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări
romania.summary: Om cu principii solide, Nicușor Dan rămâne un mediator indiferent de situația în care se regăsește. Chiar și azi, când Sorin Grindeanu l-a trimis după țigări, președintele și-a demonstrat încă o dată abilitățile de mediator și, în locul unui refuz categoric, a ales să-i medieze liderului PSD pachetul de țigări cerut. Nicușor Dan și-a mediat […] The post Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 S-a aflat adevărata identitate a şoferului lui Simion. Era Grindeanu!
romania.summary: Mai ceva ca SPP-istul lui Nicuşor Dan, misteriosul şofer al liderului AUR George Simion a stârnit constant imaginaţia a mii de bărbaţi! Astăzi, însă, identitatea acestuia ieşit la iveală – şi, culmea, e de la un partid concurent. „În tot acest timp, şoferul lui Simion era Grindeanu! Oau! Mă încearcă nişte sentimente tare dubioase acum… […] The post S-a aflat adevărata identitate a şoferului lui Simion. Era Grindeanu! appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
romania.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
romania.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

medical.title: Community-based care can cut depression and self-harm risk among teens in India's urban slums
medical.summary: In a significant advance for adolescent mental health in India, researchers have successfully demonstrated a scalable, community‑based care model through Project ARTEMIS, showing measurable reductions in depression, self‑harm and suicide risk across slum communities.

medical.title: 'Click clotting' stops bleeding fast and could transform emergency care
medical.summary: Researchers at McGill University have developed a rapid way to engineer blood clots that stop severe bleeding and support tissue healing more effectively. Their technique, called "click clotting," links red blood cell surface proteins through a chemical reaction, resulting in a biocompatible clot that is 13 times more resistant to fracturing and four times more adhesive than natural blood clots.

medical.title: Infants make nuanced moral character judgments as early as 12 months old
medical.summary: Psychologists at the University of Toronto have found that we begin to make moral character judgments as early as 12 months old. The research, published in Communications Psychology, also recognizes that individuals can exist along a moral spectrum and suggests that early social interactions may play a role in shaping infants' moral judgments.

medical.title: Nerves in skin can slow melanoma growth
medical.summary: Nerve fibers within melanomas can slow their growth, according to a study led by Weill Cornell Medicine investigators. The findings help clarify the emerging field of cancer neuroscience and may inform future therapeutic strategies.

medical.title: Validation study reveals new tool can predict patients' genetic risk of 8 cardiovascular conditions
medical.summary: Researchers at Mass General Brigham Heart and Vascular Institute and collaborators have developed and validated a new genetic risk test that can estimate a person's inherited risk for eight common cardiovascular conditions, offering a more comprehensive approach to prevention.

medical.title: Optimizing exercise and nutrition before surgery boosts patient outcomes, review finds
medical.summary: Providing patients with structured exercise and nutrition support before surgery can reduce complications and shorten hospital stays, according to a new review published in the Journal of the American College of Surgeons (JACS).

medical.title: Americans support cannabis rescheduling, study finds
medical.summary: Most people strongly support the federal government's reclassification of cannabis, according to a new study that used AI to analyze more than 40,000 comments in the public record. The findings by researchers at Johns Hopkins University and the University of California San Diego, which come as the Trump administration last week reclassified state-licensed medical marijuana from a Schedule I drug to a less dangerous Schedule III, suggest the public would like to see even more reform.

medical.title: Significant rise in valley fever cases in El Paso linked to extreme weather, dust, study finds
medical.summary: A new study by researchers at The University of Texas at El Paso has identified a significant rise in Valley fever cases in El Paso over the past decade and found strong connections between the disease and extreme weather, wind and airborne dust.

medical.title: Glioblastoma mapping uncovers four recurring tumor cell communities, revealing treatment targets
medical.summary: Glioblastoma (GBM) is an aggressive type of brain cancer that is known to be very difficult to treat. One reason why this type of cancer is often resistant to available treatments is that it is characterized by a highly diverse cellular structure and complex interactions between cancerous cells and neighboring healthy cells.

medical.title: Survey finds many women still believe mammograms should start at age 50—experts say age 40
medical.summary: A new national survey reveals many women are unsure about when to start mammogram screening for breast cancer and believe they should start later than doctors recommend.

science.title: AI discovery reveals DNA isn't locked away in cells after all
science.summary: Every cell in the human body squeezes over six feet of DNA into a minuscule speck invisible to the naked eye—like compressing a whole house into a single sugar cube. In order to fit in a cell and remain organized, DNA is carefully wrapped around spool-like protein clusters called nucleosomes.

science.title: The battle of the sexes in the egg: How early nuclear rivalry helps embryos develop properly
science.summary: The sperm and the egg cell's nuclei compete for size directly after fertilization and this is necessary for proper embryonic development. A mouse study with Kobe University participation finally gives meaning to a phenomenon biologists have known for decades.

science.title: Q&A: Prediction markets are surging—here's what you need to know
science.summary: Stanford scholar and former SEC Commissioner Joseph Grundfest shares insights on prediction markets, which are gaining popularity amid concerns over regulation and potential manipulation.

science.title: Oldest burial in Patagonia reveals early human settlement along South America's Atlantic coast
science.summary: The peopling of South America has long been debated, with various routes proposed for how they spread across the subcontinent. However, routes along the Atlantic coast were typically much younger than their Pacific counterparts, leaving a significant gap in our understanding of when and how early hunter-gatherers traveled along the South American Atlantic coast.

science.title: NASA Demonstrates New Prescribed Burn Capability for Spaceport
science.summary: Anyone who has seen a launch at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida knows the agency’s pursuit of the stars involves some smoke and fire. Sometimes, however, the smoke doesn’t come from the rockets that propel astronauts beyond Earth’s bounds. That was the case during the second weekend of January 2026, when NASA teamed up […]

science.title: Lack of education or employment in early adulthood has scarring effect into midlife, study shows
science.summary: Being out of work and education between the ages of 16 and 24 has long-term consequences for people's employment, finances, physical and mental health in midlife, according to new UCL research. A new report by the UCL Centre for Longitudinal Studies reveals that generation X experienced worse outcomes at age 51 the longer they had spent NEET (not in employment, education or training) in early adulthood. Those who were persistently NEET between ages 16 and 24 were six times as likely to be out of work in midlife than those who were never NEET in early adulthood. They were also three times as likely to report being in poor health.

science.title: Physicists reveal universal speed limit on quantum information scrambling
science.summary: Theoretical physicists in the US have discovered a "speed limit" on the time taken for quantum information to spread through larger systems. Publishing their results in Physical Review Letters, Amit Vikram and colleagues at the University of Maryland have proved for the first time that this minimum time is closely linked with a system's entropy and temperature, perhaps paving the way for a deeper understanding of quantum information across a wide range of physical settings.

science.title: CEQ Permitting Innovators Program Call for Solutions
science.summary: NASA’s Center of Excellence for Collaborative Innovation (CoECI) assists in the use of crowdsourcing across the federal government. CoECI’s NASA Tournament Lab offers the contract capability to run external crowdsourced challenges on behalf of NASA and other agencies. The Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ), within the Executive Office of the President established by the National […]

science.title: Exploring representation through digital archaeology and game design
science.summary: Recently, Michael Hall, a doctoral candidate in the Comparative Literature and Cultural Studies Program, was invited to present his work exploring representation in gaming, "Dawnbreaker, the Curious Case of Decolonialism and Colonialism in Final Fantasy XIV, Dawntrail," at the Popular Culture Association National Conference.

science.title: 'Ruthless predator' of red tide plankton reveals unusual bioluminescence
science.summary: Scientists at UC San Diego's Scripps Institution of Oceanography have uncovered new insights into the bioluminescence of a unique species of marine plankton that feeds on other plankton, including the harmful algae responsible for red tides and algal blooms.

science.title: New study details changing US irrigated agriculture, viability strategies
science.summary: A new study by researchers at the Daugherty Water for Food Global Institute at the University of Nebraska offers a comprehensive national-scale assessment of irrigated agriculture in the United States in recent years. Published in Agricultural Water Management, the findings carry important implications for the future of food production, water policy, and rural livelihoods across the country.

science.title: No brain required: This is how the single-celled Stentor learns
science.summary: Scientists have known for more than a century that a single-celled organism with no nerve cells—much less a brain—can behave in ways that resemble learning. But those observations only went so far. How the organism did that was a mystery.

science.title: Levitated nano-ferromagnet confirms a 160-year-old physical prediction
science.summary: Ferromagnets, such as iron, cobalt, and nickel, are materials with a strong, spontaneous, and permanent magnetic field. Over 150 years ago, the physicist and mathematician James Clerk Maxwell speculated that under specific conditions, non-spinning ferromagnets or electromagnets would behave as gyroscopes, objects that maintain their orientation, typically due to the angular momentum arising from spinning.

science.title: Scientists just found a chilling way life may have begun
science.summary: New experiments suggest that freezing and thawing on early Earth may have helped primitive cell-like structures grow and evolve. Tiny lipid bubbles behaved very differently depending on their membrane makeup—some fused into larger compartments and captured DNA more efficiently. These fusion events could have mixed key molecules, setting the stage for more complex chemistry.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: 😂 Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
mix_items.summary: Lansat în 2006 ca o satiră SF a unei lumi posibile, dar improbabile, filmul Idiocracy a ajuns să fie perceput azi cu totul altfel. Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Community-based care can cut depression and self-harm risk among teens in India's urban slums
mix_items.summary: In a significant advance for adolescent mental health in India, researchers have successfully demonstrated a scalable, community‑based care model through Project ARTEMIS, showing measurable reductions in depression, self‑harm and suicide risk across slum communities.

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: AI discovery reveals DNA isn't locked away in cells after all
mix_items.summary: Every cell in the human body squeezes over six feet of DNA into a minuscule speck invisible to the naked eye—like compressing a whole house into a single sugar cube. In order to fit in a cell and remain organized, DNA is carefully wrapped around spool-like protein clusters called nucleosomes.

mix_items.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
mix_items.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
mix_items.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

mix_items.title: 'Click clotting' stops bleeding fast and could transform emergency care
mix_items.summary: Researchers at McGill University have developed a rapid way to engineer blood clots that stop severe bleeding and support tissue healing more effectively. Their technique, called "click clotting," links red blood cell surface proteins through a chemical reaction, resulting in a biocompatible clot that is 13 times more resistant to fracturing and four times more adhesive than natural blood clots.

mix_items.title: The battle of the sexes in the egg: How early nuclear rivalry helps embryos develop properly
mix_items.summary: The sperm and the egg cell's nuclei compete for size directly after fertilization and this is necessary for proper embryonic development. A mouse study with Kobe University participation finally gives meaning to a phenomenon biologists have known for decades.

mix_items.title: Infants make nuanced moral character judgments as early as 12 months old
mix_items.summary: Psychologists at the University of Toronto have found that we begin to make moral character judgments as early as 12 months old. The research, published in Communications Psychology, also recognizes that individuals can exist along a moral spectrum and suggests that early social interactions may play a role in shaping infants' moral judgments.

mix_items.title: Nerves in skin can slow melanoma growth
mix_items.summary: Nerve fibers within melanomas can slow their growth, according to a study led by Weill Cornell Medicine investigators. The findings help clarify the emerging field of cancer neuroscience and may inform future therapeutic strategies.

mix_items.title: Q&A: Prediction markets are surging—here's what you need to know
mix_items.summary: Stanford scholar and former SEC Commissioner Joseph Grundfest shares insights on prediction markets, which are gaining popularity amid concerns over regulation and potential manipulation.

mix_items.title: Oldest burial in Patagonia reveals early human settlement along South America's Atlantic coast
mix_items.summary: The peopling of South America has long been debated, with various routes proposed for how they spread across the subcontinent. However, routes along the Atlantic coast were typically much younger than their Pacific counterparts, leaving a significant gap in our understanding of when and how early hunter-gatherers traveled along the South American Atlantic coast.

mix_items.title: NASA Demonstrates New Prescribed Burn Capability for Spaceport
mix_items.summary: Anyone who has seen a launch at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida knows the agency’s pursuit of the stars involves some smoke and fire. Sometimes, however, the smoke doesn’t come from the rockets that propel astronauts beyond Earth’s bounds. That was the case during the second weekend of January 2026, when NASA teamed up […]

mix_items.title: Lack of education or employment in early adulthood has scarring effect into midlife, study shows
mix_items.summary: Being out of work and education between the ages of 16 and 24 has long-term consequences for people's employment, finances, physical and mental health in midlife, according to new UCL research. A new report by the UCL Centre for Longitudinal Studies reveals that generation X experienced worse outcomes at age 51 the longer they had spent NEET (not in employment, education or training) in early adulthood. Those who were persistently NEET between ages 16 and 24 were six times as likely to be out of work in midlife than those who were never NEET in early adulthood. They were also three times as likely to report being in poor health.

mix_items.title: Physicists reveal universal speed limit on quantum information scrambling
mix_items.summary: Theoretical physicists in the US have discovered a "speed limit" on the time taken for quantum information to spread through larger systems. Publishing their results in Physical Review Letters, Amit Vikram and colleagues at the University of Maryland have proved for the first time that this minimum time is closely linked with a system's entropy and temperature, perhaps paving the way for a deeper understanding of quantum information across a wide range of physical settings.

mix_items.title: CEQ Permitting Innovators Program Call for Solutions
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Center of Excellence for Collaborative Innovation (CoECI) assists in the use of crowdsourcing across the federal government. CoECI’s NASA Tournament Lab offers the contract capability to run external crowdsourced challenges on behalf of NASA and other agencies. The Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ), within the Executive Office of the President established by the National […]

mix_items.title: Validation study reveals new tool can predict patients' genetic risk of 8 cardiovascular conditions
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Mass General Brigham Heart and Vascular Institute and collaborators have developed and validated a new genetic risk test that can estimate a person's inherited risk for eight common cardiovascular conditions, offering a more comprehensive approach to prevention.

mix_items.title: Optimizing exercise and nutrition before surgery boosts patient outcomes, review finds
mix_items.summary: Providing patients with structured exercise and nutrition support before surgery can reduce complications and shorten hospital stays, according to a new review published in the Journal of the American College of Surgeons (JACS).

mix_items.title: Exploring representation through digital archaeology and game design
mix_items.summary: Recently, Michael Hall, a doctoral candidate in the Comparative Literature and Cultural Studies Program, was invited to present his work exploring representation in gaming, "Dawnbreaker, the Curious Case of Decolonialism and Colonialism in Final Fantasy XIV, Dawntrail," at the Popular Culture Association National Conference.

mix_items.title: 'Ruthless predator' of red tide plankton reveals unusual bioluminescence
mix_items.summary: Scientists at UC San Diego's Scripps Institution of Oceanography have uncovered new insights into the bioluminescence of a unique species of marine plankton that feeds on other plankton, including the harmful algae responsible for red tides and algal blooms.

mix_items.title: New study details changing US irrigated agriculture, viability strategies
mix_items.summary: A new study by researchers at the Daugherty Water for Food Global Institute at the University of Nebraska offers a comprehensive national-scale assessment of irrigated agriculture in the United States in recent years. Published in Agricultural Water Management, the findings carry important implications for the future of food production, water policy, and rural livelihoods across the country.

mix_items.title: No brain required: This is how the single-celled Stentor learns
mix_items.summary: Scientists have known for more than a century that a single-celled organism with no nerve cells—much less a brain—can behave in ways that resemble learning. But those observations only went so far. How the organism did that was a mystery.

mix_items.title: Americans support cannabis rescheduling, study finds
mix_items.summary: Most people strongly support the federal government's reclassification of cannabis, according to a new study that used AI to analyze more than 40,000 comments in the public record. The findings by researchers at Johns Hopkins University and the University of California San Diego, which come as the Trump administration last week reclassified state-licensed medical marijuana from a Schedule I drug to a less dangerous Schedule III, suggest the public would like to see even more reform.

mix_items.title: Levitated nano-ferromagnet confirms a 160-year-old physical prediction
mix_items.summary: Ferromagnets, such as iron, cobalt, and nickel, are materials with a strong, spontaneous, and permanent magnetic field. Over 150 years ago, the physicist and mathematician James Clerk Maxwell speculated that under specific conditions, non-spinning ferromagnets or electromagnets would behave as gyroscopes, objects that maintain their orientation, typically due to the angular momentum arising from spinning.

mix_items.title: Significant rise in valley fever cases in El Paso linked to extreme weather, dust, study finds
mix_items.summary: A new study by researchers at The University of Texas at El Paso has identified a significant rise in Valley fever cases in El Paso over the past decade and found strong connections between the disease and extreme weather, wind and airborne dust.

mix_items.title: Glioblastoma mapping uncovers four recurring tumor cell communities, revealing treatment targets
mix_items.summary: Glioblastoma (GBM) is an aggressive type of brain cancer that is known to be very difficult to treat. One reason why this type of cancer is often resistant to available treatments is that it is characterized by a highly diverse cellular structure and complex interactions between cancerous cells and neighboring healthy cells.

mix_items.title: Survey finds many women still believe mammograms should start at age 50—experts say age 40
mix_items.summary: A new national survey reveals many women are unsure about when to start mammogram screening for breast cancer and believe they should start later than doctors recommend.

mix_items.title: Scientists just found a chilling way life may have begun
mix_items.summary: New experiments suggest that freezing and thawing on early Earth may have helped primitive cell-like structures grow and evolve. Tiny lipid bubbles behaved very differently depending on their membrane makeup—some fused into larger compartments and captured DNA more efficiently. These fusion events could have mixed key molecules, setting the stage for more complex chemistry.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce plan ai pe azi? - Sa nu stric planul de ieri.

satire_ro.title: Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
